name: Gather / Scatter

x.executionModel: SIMD instructions that load (gather) or store (scatter) elements from/to non-contiguous memory addresses given by a vector of indices, in one instruction.
x.dataOrg: Index/offset vector + a base; effective when data is irregular but still vectorisable (sparse, indexed, indirect access).
x.hwConstraints: Throughput far below contiguous loads; each element may touch a different cache line / TLB entry.
x.compilerSupport: Auto-generated for indexed loops on capable ISAs (AVX2/AVX-512 gather, SVE); also exposed via intrinsics.
x.portability: Availability and performance vary widely by ISA generation; some scatter only on newer hardware.
x.expectedBenefit: Vectorises code that would otherwise fall back to scalar due to non-unit-stride access.
bottlenecks: Multiple cache lines / TLB misses per instruction
Slower than contiguous SIMD by large factors
x.workloadShapes: Sparse linear algebra, indexed lookups, particle/graph data, AoS field extraction.

def: SIMD load/store instructions that move elements to or from scattered (index-specified) memory locations in a single operation, extending vectorisation to irregular access.
problem: Classic SIMD needs contiguous, unit-stride data; many real workloads access memory through indices or sparse layouts.
mechanism: A vector of indices selects the addresses; gather reads those elements into a SIMD register, scatter writes register lanes back to them. This keeps a loop vectorised despite non-contiguous access, at the cost of touching many cache lines.
benefits: Enables SIMD over indexed / sparse data
Avoids scalar fallback for indirect access
One instruction replaces a lane-by-lane loop
limits: Much lower throughput than contiguous SIMD
Hardware support and speed vary by ISA
failures: Assuming gather is as fast as a packed load
Scatter with duplicate indices producing undefined ordering
OOB indices faulting or corrupting memory
whenNot: Data can be reorganised to contiguous/SoA (do that instead)
Memory-bandwidth-bound kernels where gather adds no win
myths: "Gather makes any loop vectorisable and fast" — it vectorises it, but is often memory-bound and slow
